Geometric Interpretation
[[span]]of the Arithmetic Operations[[/span]]
Addition and Subtraction

Geometrically, addition of two complex numbers @@math:13:ad758c@@ and @@math:14:8e1619@@ can be visualized as
                    addition of the vectors by using the [[em]]parallelogram law[[/em]]. The vector sum
                    @@math:15:7f9e50@@ is represented by the diagonal of the parallelogram formed by the two
                    original vectors.

The easiest way to represent the difference @@math:16:e0d37f@@ is to think in terms of
                    adding a negative vector @@math:17:418506@@ The negative vector is the same vector
                    as its positive counterpart, only pointing in the opposite direction.

Use the following applet to explore this geometric interpretation. Activate the boxes below to
                    show the addition or subtraction. You can also drag the points @@math:18:52fa1c@@ and @@math:19:62c247@@ around.

[[strong]]Exercise 1:[[/strong]] Can you think about a geometric interpretation of the addition
                        of three complex numbers? In general, what would be a geometric interpretation of the addition
                        of
                        @@math:20:cf2774@@ complex numbers?

Thus the product @@math:25:d57aed@@ has the modulus @@math:26:6b883a@@ and the argument @@math:27:2f3778@@

In the following applet, you can appreciate what happens to the argument of the product.
                    Drag the points @@math:28:4358c3@@ and @@math:29:07c6db@@ around and observe the behaviour of the angles.
                    Then drag the slider below.

Multiplication of complex numbers as stretching (squeezing) and rotation

In the applet below a set of points are defined randomly on the complex plane. Then each point is multiplied by a given complex number @@math:32:91d27c@@ On the right-side screen, drag around the point @@math:33:574353@@ and analyze the behaviour of the points (⭕) multiplied by @@math:34:e3e7de@@ and try to answer the following questions:
  • What happens when @@math:35:987348@@ is inside, or outside, the unit circle?
  • What happens if @@math:36:ce6faf@@ moves only around the unit circle?
[[strong]]Note: [[/strong]]You can also study the behaviour of the points (⚫) multiplied by @@math:37:d83298@@ by activating the box [[code]]Multiply by 1/z[[/code]].

As you already have noticed, the geometric interpretation of multiplication of complex numbers is
                    stretching
                    (or squeezing) and rotation of vectors in the plane.

In the previous applet, with the option [[code]]Multiply by z[[/code]], set [[code]]n = 1[[/code]] by dragging the slider to the left side. In this case, the applet shows the three complex numbers @@math:11:9d0399@@ represented as vectors. When @@math:38:d985fe@@ and @@math:39:8869e3@@ are non zero, then
  • the modulus of @@math:40:de67e6@@ is equal to @@math:41:ab633d@@ and
  • the argument of @@math:42:e35875@@ is equal to @@math:43:a18052@@
If @@math:44:93afd5@@ we deal with stretching. If @@math:45:94f59b@@ it is a case of squeezing.

@@verbatim:0:3f0c52@@

Contents

  • Addition and Subtraction
  • Multiplication
  • Stretching and rotation
    • @@verbatim:1:6ff3ce@@


Geometric Interpretation

of the Arithmetic Operations

Addition and Subtraction

Geometrically, addition of two complex numbers @@math:13:ad758c@@ and @@math:14:8e1619@@ can be visualized as addition of the vectors by using the parallelogram law. The vector sum @@math:15:7f9e50@@ is represented by the diagonal of the parallelogram formed by the two original vectors.

The easiest way to represent the difference @@math:16:e0d37f@@ is to think in terms of adding a negative vector @@math:17:418506@@ The negative vector is the same vector as its positive counterpart, only pointing in the opposite direction.

Use the following applet to explore this geometric interpretation. Activate the boxes below to show the addition or subtraction. You can also drag the points @@math:18:52fa1c@@ and @@math:19:62c247@@ around.

Exercise 1: Can you think about a geometric interpretation of the addition of three complex numbers? In general, what would be a geometric interpretation of the addition of @@math:20:cf2774@@ complex numbers?

Multiplication

In the previous section we defined the multiplication of two complex numbers @@math:21:f8757a@@ and @@math:22:fa19ee@@ as

@@math:6:40efcc@@
In this case, to appreciate what happens geometrically we need to consider the polar form of @@math:23:d45750@@ and @@math:24:7e63d6@@ That is
@@math:7:ea4f62@@
Then the product can be written in the form
@@math:8:5bb23b@@
Now by means of the addition theorems of the sine and cosine this expression can be simplified to
@@math:9:619f64@@
Thus the product @@math:25:d57aed@@ has the modulus @@math:26:6b883a@@ and the argument @@math:27:2f3778@@

In the following applet, you can appreciate what happens to the argument of the product. Drag the points @@math:28:4358c3@@ and @@math:29:07c6db@@ around and observe the behaviour of the angles. Then drag the slider below.

Exercise 2: Consider now @@math:10:c50cd9@@ such that @@math:30:083c80@@ Find the polar representation of @@math:31:17a59c@@ What is the geometric interpretation of this expression?

Multiplication of complex numbers as stretching (squeezing) and rotation

In the applet below a set of points are defined randomly on the complex plane. Then each point is multiplied by a given complex number @@math:32:91d27c@@ On the right-side screen, drag around the point @@math:33:574353@@ and analyze the behaviour of the points (⭕) multiplied by @@math:34:e3e7de@@ and try to answer the following questions:

  • What happens when @@math:35:987348@@ is inside, or outside, the unit circle?
  • What happens if @@math:36:ce6faf@@ moves only around the unit circle?
Note: You can also study the behaviour of the points (⚫) multiplied by @@math:37:d83298@@ by activating the box Multiply by 1/z.

As you already have noticed, the geometric interpretation of multiplication of complex numbers is stretching (or squeezing) and rotation of vectors in the plane.

In the previous applet, with the option Multiply by z, set n = 1 by dragging the slider to the left side. In this case, the applet shows the three complex numbers @@math:11:9d0399@@ represented as vectors. When @@math:38:d985fe@@ and @@math:39:8869e3@@ are non zero, then

  • the modulus of @@math:40:de67e6@@ is equal to @@math:41:ab633d@@ and
  • the argument of @@math:42:e35875@@ is equal to @@math:43:a18052@@
If @@math:44:93afd5@@ we deal with stretching. If @@math:45:94f59b@@ it is a case of squeezing.

Exercise 3: Use the same applet, with the option Multiply by 1/z, to investigate what happens when we multiply by @@math:46:ef8910@@ Set n = 1 by dragging the slider to the left side to show the three complex numbers @@math:12:3d2d77@@ What happens to the modulus and argument of @@math:47:bd7da6@@?
